title: The irreducible vectors of a lattice: Some theory and applications
overall_summary: The paper introduces an additive notion of irreducibility for full-rank Euclidean lattices: a nonzero lattice vector is paper-irreducible if it cannot be written as a sum of two lattice vectors both strictly shorter than it. It proves that shortest vectors and certain successive-minimum vectors are paper-irreducible, gives an exact norm and inner-product characterization of paper-irreducibility, shows paper-irreducible vectors generate the lattice, and proves that every paper-irreducible vector is Voronoi relevant. Independently of the paper's new notion, its recalled Voronoi theorem is especially useful: a vector is relevant exactly when ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for all lattice vectors x other than 0 and v. For the original problem, this is likely the most important bridge, because the original decomposition obstruction u=a+b with a·b≥0 is algebraically the negation of that relevant-vector inequality after taking x=a.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1 (relevant vectors define the Voronoi cell minimally)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n with Euclidean norm ∥·∥. Let V(L)={x∈R^n : ∥x∥≤∥x−y∥ for every y∈L} be its Voronoi cell at the origin, and for v∈L define H(v)={x∈R^n : ∥x∥≤∥x−v∥}. Let R(L) be the set of nonzero lattice vectors r such that V(L) and r+V(L) share a non-empty boundary. Then R(L) is the minimal subset A⊂L such that V(L)=∩_{v∈A} H(v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality V(L)=∩_{y∈L}H(y) follows immediately from the definition of V(L). The relevant vectors are precisely those lattice vectors whose bisector inequality H(r) actually supports a boundary piece shared by the Voronoi cell of 0 and the Voronoi cell of r; such inequalities cannot be removed without enlarging the intersection. Conversely, if a nonzero lattice vector is not relevant, its inequality does not define a boundary piece of the Voronoi cell and is redundant once the inequalities from relevant vectors are imposed. This gives both the intersection formula using R(L) and minimality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (Voronoi criterion for relevant vectors)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n, and let v∈L\{0}. Then v∈R(L) if and only if 0 and v are the only closest lattice vectors of L to the point v/2. Equivalently, R(L)={v∈L\{0} : ∥v/2−x∥>∥v/2∥ for every x∈L\{0,v}}={v∈L\{0} : ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for every x∈L\{0,v}}. Moreover, to check the inequality ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² it is enough to check lattice vectors x with ∥x∥<∥v∥.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes the Voronoi characterization: v is relevant exactly when the midpoint v/2 has exactly two closest lattice points, namely 0 and v. The first displayed description is just this closest-vector condition, since the distance from v/2 to both 0 and v equals ∥v/2∥. Expanding ∥v/2−x∥²>∥v/2∥² gives ∥x∥²−⟨v,x⟩>0, hence ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥². The restriction to ∥x∥<∥v∥ uses Cauchy-Schwarz: if ∥x∥≥∥v∥ and x≠v, then ⟨v,x⟩≤∥v∥∥x∥≤∥x∥², and equality in both inequalities would force x=v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1 (witness for non-relevance)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. The paper states that if v∈L and v∉R(L), then there exists r∈R(L) such that ⟨v,r⟩≥∥r∥²; the meaningful nonzero case is v≠0, since 0 is not relevant and cannot satisfy the conclusion. In applications, this says that a non-relevant nonzero vector has a relevant vector witnessing failure of the strict inequality in Theorem 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as a known lemma from Micciancio and Voulgaris. A geometric reconstruction is as follows. Since relevant vectors give all irredundant halfspace inequalities for V(L), a point y lies in V(L) exactly when 2⟨y,r⟩≤∥r∥² for every r∈R(L). Apply this to y=v/2. If every relevant r satisfied ⟨v,r⟩<∥r∥², then v/2 would lie in the interior of the Voronoi cell. But v/2 is equidistant from 0 and v, so for nonzero v it cannot be an interior point for which 0 is strictly closer than every other lattice point. Hence some relevant r has ⟨v,r⟩≥∥r∥².

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2 (shortest vectors are relevant)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Let λ_1(L)=min{∥x∥ : x∈L\{0}} and S_1(L)={v∈L : ∥v∥=λ_1(L)} be the first shell. Then S_1(L)⊆R(L).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the inequality characterization of relevant vectors. Let v∈S_1(L). For any x∈L\{0,v}, there is no nonzero lattice vector shorter than v. If ∥x∥>∥v∥, then ⟨v,x⟩≤∥v∥∥x∥<∥x∥². If ∥x∥=∥v∥ and x≠v, then Cauchy-Schwarz is strict unless x is a positive scalar multiple of v; since the norms are equal, equality would force x=v, excluded. Thus ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for all x≠0,v, so v is relevant by Theorem 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3 (relevant vectors generate)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Then there exists a generating set G of L such that G⊆R(L). Equivalently, the subgroup generated by R(L) is all of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take any nonzero vector y∈L. If y is relevant, subtract y and reach 0. If y is not relevant, Lemma 1 gives r∈R(L) with ⟨y,r⟩≥∥r∥². Then ∥y−r∥²=∥y∥²+∥r∥²−2⟨y,r⟩≤∥y∥²−∥r∥²<∥y∥², so replacing y by y−r strictly decreases the norm. Since a lattice has only finitely many points in a bounded ball, repeated reductions terminate at 0. Reversing the reductions writes the original y as an integer sum of relevant vectors. Hence R(L) spans L as an abelian group, and a generating subset G⊆R(L) may be chosen.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4 and Lemma 3 (paper-irreducibility and shortest vectors)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's terminology, a nonzero vector v∈L is irreducible if there do not exist v_1,v_2∈L such that v=v_1+v_2 and ∥v_i∥<∥v∥ for i=1,2. Let Irr(L) be the set of such vectors. For any full-rank lattice L in R^n, S_1(L)⊆Irr(L), where S_1(L) is the set of shortest nonzero lattice vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let v be shortest, so ∥v∥=λ_1(L). If v=v_1+v_2 with ∥v_i∥<∥v∥ for both i, then each v_i has norm strictly less than λ_1(L). Since λ_1(L) is the smallest positive norm in L, both v_i must be 0. This contradicts v≠0. Therefore no such decomposition exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4 (successive-minimum vector outside the shorter sublattice is paper-irreducible)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. For λ>0, let L_λ be the sublattice spanned by all lattice vectors of norm strictly less than λ. Let λ_i(L) be the i-th successive minimum. If v∈L satisfies ∥v∥=λ_i(L) for some 1≤i≤n and v∉L_{λ_i(L)}, then v∈Irr(L) in the paper's sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The case i=1 is Lemma 3. For i≥2, suppose v is not paper-irreducible. Then v=v_1+v_2 with both ∥v_j∥<∥v∥=λ_i(L). Hence v_1 and v_2 both lie in L_{λ_i(L)} by definition of that shorter-vector sublattice. Since L_{λ_i(L)} is a subgroup, v=v_1+v_2 lies in L_{λ_i(L)}, contradicting the hypothesis v∉L_{λ_i(L)}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1 (paper-irreducibles attain all successive minima)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. For every i=1,...,n, there exists v∈Irr(L) such that ∥v∥=λ_i(L).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 4 it suffices, for each i, to find a vector of norm λ_i(L) that is not in the sublattice L_{λ_i(L)} spanned by shorter vectors. Let k be the smallest index with λ_k(L)=λ_i(L), so λ_{k−1}(L)<λ_k(L)=λ_i(L), with the convention λ_0(L)=0. The shorter-vector sublattice L_{λ_i(L)} has rank k−1: there are k−1 independent vectors of norm below λ_i(L), but not k. If every vector of norm λ_i(L) lay in L_{λ_i(L)}, then all vectors of norm at most λ_i(L) would lie in a rank k−1 sublattice, making it impossible to find k linearly independent vectors of norm at most λ_k(L). This contradicts the definition of λ_k(L). Therefore some norm-λ_i vector lies outside L_{λ_i(L)}, and Proposition 4 makes it paper-irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5 (paper-irreducible vectors generate)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Then there exists a generating set G of L such that G⊆Irr(L). Equivalently, the subgroup generated by the paper-irreducible vectors is all of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take any v∈L. If v is paper-irreducible, it is already in Irr(L). Otherwise write v=v_1+v_2 with ∥v_i∥<∥v∥. For each summand that is not paper-irreducible, decompose it again into two strictly shorter lattice vectors. At every branch the norm strictly decreases, and there are only finitely many lattice points in the closed ball B(0,∥v∥). Thus the recursive decomposition terminates after finitely many steps. The final leaves are paper-irreducible vectors whose sum is v. Hence every lattice vector is an integer sum of paper-irreducibles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4 (characterization of paper-irreducible vectors)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. A nonzero vector v∈L is paper-irreducible if and only if for every x∈L with ∥x∥<∥v∥ one has ∥v−x∥≥∥v∥. Equivalently, v∈Irr(L) if and only if for every x∈L with ∥x∥<∥v∥ one has 2⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥².
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p is paper-irreducible and x is shorter than p, then ∥p−x∥ cannot also be shorter than p, because p=x+(p−x) would be a decomposition into two shorter lattice vectors. Thus ∥p−x∥≥∥p∥. Conversely, suppose v has the stated norm property. In any decomposition v=v_1+v_2, if ∥v_1∥<∥v∥ then the property with x=v_1 gives ∥v_2∥=∥v−v_1∥≥∥v∥, so the decomposition is not into two shorter vectors. If ∥v_1∥≥∥v∥, it is also not a two-shorter decomposition. Therefore v is paper-irreducible. The inner-product form is obtained by expanding ∥v−x∥²≥∥v∥², which is equivalent to ∥x∥²−2⟨v,x⟩≥0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6 (paper-irreducible vectors are relevant)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Then every paper-irreducible vector of L is a relevant vector: Irr(L)⊆R(L). Consequently Irr(L) is finite.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Theorem 1 and Lemma 4. It is enough to show that if v is paper-irreducible and x∈L\{0} has ∥x∥<∥v∥, then ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥². Lemma 4 gives 2⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥². If ⟨v,x⟩≤0, then ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² because x≠0. If ⟨v,x⟩>0, then ⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥²/2<∥x∥². By the shorter-vector reduction in Theorem 1, this proves v∈R(L). Finiteness follows because R(L) is finite.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 7 and 8 (complete systems of paper-irreducible vectors generate and are angle-separated)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Partition Irr(L) into equivalence classes by equal norm. For each class S choose a subset tilde S such that if p∈tilde S then −p∈tilde S, and tilde S is maximal subject to the condition that for every p,q∈tilde S with q≠−p one has ∥p+q∥≥∥p∥. Define P(L)=∪_S tilde S, called a complete system of irreducible vectors. Then every such P(L) contains a generating set of L. Moreover, if p_1,p_2∈P(L) and p_1≠±p_2, then min{∥p_1+p_2∥,∥p_1−p_2∥}≥max{∥p_1∥,∥p_2∥}; consequently, if θ is the Euclidean angle between p_1 and p_2, then |cos θ|≤1/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For generation, order the finitely many norm classes C_1,...,C_t by increasing norm, and define A_i=(∪_{j<i}C_j)∪(∪_{j≥i}tilde C_j). Then A_1=P(L) and A_{t+1}=Irr(L), and Irr(L) spans L by Proposition 5. Descend on i. If A_k spans, the only vectors removed when passing to A_{k−1} are those in C_{k−1}\tilde C_{k−1}. For such a vector v, maximality of tilde C_{k−1} gives a representative tilde v∈tilde C_{k−1} such that, after choosing the appropriate sign, w=v+tilde v has ∥w∥<∥v∥. The shorter vector w is either paper-irreducible of a smaller class or decomposes into still shorter paper-irreducibles, all of which lie in A_{k−1}; hence v=w−tilde v is generated by A_{k−1}. Thus P(L) spans. For the separation property, assume ∥p_2∥≤∥p_1∥. If the norms are unequal, Lemma 4 applied to the longer paper-irreducible p_1 with x=±p_2 gives ∥p_1∓p_2∥≥∥p_1∥. If the norms are equal, the defining condition of the chosen representative set gives the same conclusion for sums, and sign symmetry gives it for differences. Expanding ∥p_1±p_2∥²≥∥p_1∥² yields 2|⟨p_1,p_2⟩|≤∥p_2∥²; dividing by ∥p_1∥∥p_2∥ gives |cos θ|≤1/2.

proof_techniques.name: Voronoi relevance as an additive irreducibility test
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls the classical Voronoi criterion that v is relevant exactly when ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for all x∈L\{0,v}. This converts a geometric statement about adjacent Voronoi cells, or about the midpoint v/2 having only 0 and v as closest lattice points, into a purely algebraic inequality against every other lattice vector.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original positive definite lattice, u=a+b with a·b≥0 is equivalent to taking x=a and obtaining u·x≥x². Thus the original irreducibility of a vertex u is exactly the relevant-vector inequality from Theorem 1, after realizing the positive definite form as a Euclidean lattice.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction by relevant vectors
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the fact that if a nonzero vector is not relevant, then some relevant vector r satisfies ⟨v,r⟩≥∥r∥². Subtracting r strictly decreases norm, since ∥v−r∥²=∥v∥²+∥r∥²−2⟨v,r⟩≤∥v∥²−∥r∥². Iterating gives a finite descent to 0 and proves that relevant vectors generate the lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one assumes all tree vertices are reducible/non-relevant, this reduction mechanism supplies relevant witnesses and strictly decreasing norm replacements. Such a descent might be combined with the tree basis and the bad-vertex hypothesis to force a contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Norm-descent decomposition into paper-irreducibles
proof_techniques.description: For the paper's stronger irreducibility notion, any reducible vector splits as a sum of two strictly shorter lattice vectors. Repeating this split must terminate because a lattice has finitely many points in any ball, so every lattice vector is a sum of paper-irreducible vectors. This is a well-founded induction on norm rather than on coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a positive definite lattice, any original decomposition with a·b≥0 automatically has both summands strictly shorter than u. Therefore proving a vertex is paper-irreducible would be sufficient for the original problem, and norm-descent arguments may help organize contradictions.

proof_techniques.name: Testing irreducibility by inequalities against shorter vectors
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4 replaces the existential condition of paper-reducibility by a universal inequality: v is paper-irreducible iff every shorter lattice vector x satisfies 2⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥². The proof is just the expansion of ∥v−x∥≥∥v∥, but it is powerful because only shorter x need be checked.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph lattice, local control of dot products u·x and norms x² may be easier than direct control of all decompositions. The analogous relevant-vector test for the original notion is u·x<x² for all x≠0,u, and Theorem 1 says longer x can often be discarded by Cauchy-Schwarz.

proof_techniques.name: Successive-minimum outside-span criterion
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4 shows that a vector attaining a successive minimum is paper-irreducible provided it does not lie in the sublattice generated by strictly shorter vectors. The corollary proves that every successive minimum is attained by some paper-irreducible vector by a rank argument on the shorter-vector sublattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree hypotheses can be used to identify a vertex that realizes a new successive minimum or is outside the span of all shorter vectors, the paper immediately gives paper-irreducibility, hence relevance/original irreducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Angle-separated complete systems
proof_techniques.description: The paper selects, from equal-norm classes of paper-irreducibles, sign-symmetric maximal representative sets whose non-opposite pairwise sums are not shorter. The union P(L) still spans the lattice and has strong pairwise separation: for distinct non-opposite p,q∈P(L), both p+q and p−q have norm at least max{∥p∥,∥q∥}, so |cos θ(p,q)|≤1/2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This may be useful if a proof needs a small or well-separated generating set of relevant or irreducible vectors and then compares it with the distinguished vertex basis of a tree lattice.

other_useful_info: Terminology caution: the paper's irreducible vectors are not the same as the original problem's irreducible elements; the paper's notion forbids all decompositions into two strictly shorter vectors, while the original notion forbids decompositions with nonnegative cross term. The paper proves the chain S_1(L)⊆Irr(L)⊆R(L), and for any complete system P(L), S_1(L)⊆P(L)⊆Irr(L)⊆R(L). It also recalls Minkowski's bound |R(L)|≤2(2^n−1), conjectures that Irr(L) contains a basis, and proves that complete systems P(L) generate and satisfy |P(L)|<2^{0.402n}; these are mostly structural rather than directly tailored to the tree problem. Appendix A gives examples showing that the inclusions S_1(L)⊆Irr(L)⊆R(L) can be strict or equal in different ways for special lattices such as D_n^* and A_n^*, and it references Conway–Sloane obtuse-superbasis results in that context.
why_relevant: Studies a closely related general notion of irreducible lattice vector and connects it to Voronoi-relevant vectors and obtuse superbases. Although the definition is not identical, its lattice-geometric tools may support an independent proof strategy.
